component_identification.content: One ghost screen, vision cards with surreal artwork, suspect/location/object card sets, clairvoyancy track and tokens, clock board for seven rounds, psychic sleeves/stands, and crow tokens for optional redraws. One player is the silent ghost while others are psychics interpreting clues.

core_game_loop.content: Across seven rounds, the ghost gives each psychic one or more vision cards to hint their assigned suspect, location, and object in sequence. Psychics discuss and secretly choose guesses. Correct guesses advance that psychic to the next stage. After all psychics finish or time runs out, the final vision determines one shared culprit combination chosen via voting.

detailed_rules.content: Setup depends on player count; lay out a tableau of suspects, locations, and objects. The ghost secretly assigns one full combination to each psychic behind the screen. During each round, the ghost may send any number of vision cards to each psychic, aiming for thematic/artistic associations rather than literal symbols. Psychics may talk freely and help interpret clues but submit personal guesses. When a psychic guesses correctly, they lock in and wait for others while gaining clairvoyancy opportunities from correct predictions of other players. If wrong, they try again in a later round. After stage three (object) all completed psychics have candidate combinations on the table. In the finale, the ghost reveals a small set of final vision cards tied to one selected combination. Psychics vote secretly; stronger clairvoyancy grants stronger votes. Majority or weighted success wins the game.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Mysterium is fully cooperative: either all psychics correctly identify the final suspect-location-object combination before the game ends, or everyone loses. Clairvoyancy does not create individual winners; it only improves voting power in the final decision.

beginner_strategy.content: As ghost, tailor clue style to each player: some read color, others read narrative or composition. Avoid overloading with too many vision cards unless necessary; a clean two-card clue is often better than five noisy ones. As psychics, explain why you think a clue fits so others can spot patterns in the ghost’s intent. Track recurring motifs (water, fire, age, motion) across rounds. Use crows sparingly for truly unusable clue hands.
